title: W9: Vision Foundation Models: Autoencoders and Manifolds
blurb: High priority. The vision section appears every year, often starting from autoencoders and the manifold hypothesis.

knowledge_points.title: Autoencoders for Representation Learning
knowledge_points.recap: Autoencoders train on unlabeled images by reconstructing inputs. The encoder can then be reused as a feature extractor for a classifier.

questions.q: 3. You want to train an image classifier but only have a large unlabeled dataset and a small labeled dataset. Describe how you would use an autoencoder to train the classifier. (3 points)
questions.answer: Train an autoencoder on the **large unlabeled dataset**, where the encoder compresses the input into a latent representation and the decoder is trained to reconstruct it **(1 point)**. **Discard the decoder and attach a classification head to the pretrained encoder (1 point)**. Fine-tune the resulting classifier on the **small labeled dataset (1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: unsupervised pretrain encoder with reconstruction, then supervised finetune encoder + classifier head.

questions.q: 1. Why is the dimensionality of the latent space $z$ in autoencoders typically smaller than the dimensionality of the input $x$? (1 point)
questions.answer: The latent space $z$ should **capture the essential factors of variation within the data**, achieved through dimensionality reduction, to ensure **a compact and meaningful representation (1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: latent bottleneck forces compression of essential factors, not pixel-level copying.

questions.q: Why can an autoencoder trained only with reconstruction loss learn features that are not optimal for classification?
questions.answer: Reconstruction loss rewards preserving all information needed to reproduce the input, including background, texture, or nuisance variation. Classification may need invariance to those details and focus on semantic class information. Therefore good reconstruction does not automatically imply a linearly separable or task-optimal representation.
questions.extend: Memory: reconstruction features and discriminative features are related but not identical.

questions.q: What happens if an autoencoder bottleneck is too large and the decoder is very powerful?
questions.answer: The model may learn an almost identity-like mapping and copy low-level details through the latent code. Reconstruction error can be low, but the latent representation may be less compressed and less semantically useful. A bottleneck or regularization is needed to encourage meaningful structure.
questions.extend: Memory: too much capacity can make reconstruction easy without learning useful compression.

knowledge_points.title: Latent Space and Natural Images
knowledge_points.recap: Latent spaces are intended to parameterize meaningful image variations; raw pixel interpolation can leave the natural-image manifold.

questions.q: 1. Let $x\in\mathbb{R}^D$ denote an image in pixel space, $z\in\mathbb{R}^d$ with $d\ll D$ a latent representation, and $G:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^D$ a learned decoder. Given two semantically different images $A$ and $B$, consider two interpolations: (i) linear interpolation in pixel space $x_{interp}=0.5x_A+0.5x_B$; (ii) linear interpolation in latent space $z_{interp}=0.5z_A+0.5z_B$, decoded via $G(z_{interp})$. Which interpolation is more likely to produce a natural-looking image? Explain. (2 points)
questions.answer: **Latent space interpolation (ii) is more likely to produce a natural-looking image (1 point).** According to the manifold hypothesis, natural images lie on a low-dimensional manifold embedded in high-dimensional pixel space. Interpolating directly in pixel space moves through regions off this manifold, producing unnatural results. Latent space interpolation stays on the manifold, since the latent space was learned to parameterize it, and thus produces semantically meaningful intermediate images **(1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: pixel interpolation leaves the natural-image manifold; latent interpolation is more likely to stay on it.

questions.q: 2. Suppose you train two autoencoders with encoders $E$ for an image reconstruction task. Autoencoder A is trained on cartoon images of dogs with latent dimension $k_A$. Autoencoder B is trained on natural photographs of dogs with latent dimension $k_B$. Assume both autoencoders achieve similar reconstruction quality on their own train sets. Under the manifold hypothesis, is $k_A$ likely smaller than, equal to, or greater than $k_B$? Justify your answer. (2 points)
questions.answer: Under the manifold hypothesis, data lies on a lower-dimensional manifold inside the high-dimensional input space. **Cartoon images are usually simpler and more uniform**, with fewer textures, colors, and variations, so they can be represented with fewer features. Natural photographs have higher-frequency details and require more features. Therefore **$k_A$ is likely smaller than $k_B$**.
questions.extend: Memory: simpler data manifold -> lower latent dimension for comparable reconstruction quality.

questions.q: What does it mean to say natural images lie near a low-dimensional manifold in pixel space?
questions.answer: Although an image has many pixels, valid natural images occupy only a tiny structured subset of all possible pixel arrays. The underlying factors such as object identity, pose, lighting, and texture have lower intrinsic dimension than the raw pixel space. A latent model tries to parameterize this structured subset.
questions.extend: Memory: pixel dimension is huge; natural-image degrees of freedom are structured and lower-dimensional.

questions.q: Why can linear interpolation in pixel space look worse than interpolation in a learned latent space?
questions.answer: Pixel-space interpolation averages raw intensities and may pass through blurry images that are not natural. A learned latent space can organize semantic factors so interpolation stays closer to the data manifold, producing smoother changes in identity, pose, or style.
questions.extend: Memory: latent interpolation can follow the manifold; pixel interpolation often cuts through off-manifold averages.
